segments.s504-g41: کنار یکی is a prepositional phrase meaning “beside one (of them)” with کنار as the preposition/adverb “beside/alongside” and یکی as the numeral/pronoun “one.” پر ز یاقوت is a nominal predicate “full of rubies” where پر = “full (of)” takes the poetic preposition ز = “of/from” and یاقوت = “ruby”; بود is the past copula “was,” placing the whole clause in past time.
segments.s504-g42: یکی را is topical/emphatic (را here highlights ‘one (of them)’). پر از گوهر is another predicate “full of gems” (پر = “full,” از = the normal preposition “of”), and نابسود is a poetic adjective qualifying the gems (here: “imperishable / not decayed”). The clause is elliptical: ‘(and) one (other) was full of imperishable gems.’
segments.s504-g43: پشیمان شد is a verbal phrase ‘became regretful’ (پشیمان adjective + شد past of شدن). آنکس که introduces a relative clause ‘the one who…’, کم داشت اوی means ‘had little’ (کم = insufficient, داشت = had) and اوی is the third‑person pronoun referring back to that person. Word order is predicate then relative clause providing the person described.
segments.s504-g44: زبرجد is a material/agent noun placed before the verb to specify what the thorn was of (“of peridot”). چنان ‘thus/so’ modifies خار ‘thorn,’ and بگذاشت is the simple past ‘he put/placed’ (ب‑ is the classical preverb). اوی again is the pronoun “he.” The clause reads literally ‘of peridot he thus placed a thorn (in it).’
segments.s504-g45: پشیمان‌تر is the comparative form ‘more regretful.’ آنکس که introduces the relative clause ‘the one who…’; خود here emphasizes ‘himself/for himself.’ برنداشت is past tense of برداشتن ‘to take/lift’ — in context it is read as a failure to take for oneself (i.e. he didn’t secure it for himself), which is why this person is described as even more regretful.
segments.s504-g46: ازان = از + آن ‘from that/of that’; گوهر پربها is a noun phrase ‘the costly/valuable gem’ (پربها = precious). سر بگاشت is a classical expression ‘he set (it) on his head’ where سر = ‘head’ and بگاشت (ب‑ + گذاشت) = ‘placed/put.’ Together: ‘from that costly gem he placed (an ornament) upon his head.’
segments.s504-g47: دو هفته is a temporal phrase ‘two weeks.’ بر آن جایگه is a locative phrase ‘at that place/spot’ (بر = ‘on/at’ + آن جایگه = ‘that spot’). بر بماند is a classical/periphrastic verbal form where بماند (subjunctive/past‑root form of ماندن) is preceded by the preverb بر (intensive/locational), giving ‘remained/stayed.’ The whole clause: ‘(they) remained at that place for two weeks.’
segments.s504-g48: چو is a conjunction here meaning ‘when.’ آسوده‌تر is comparative ‘more at ease/relieved,’ and گشت is past of شدن ‘became.’ لشکر is the subject ‘the army/host’ and براند (راند + بر‑) is a classical verb meaning ‘to drive/lead/put in motion’ (here: ‘moved on’ or ‘marched forth’). So: ‘When the army became more at ease, it moved on.’
segments.s504-g25: وگر introduces a conditional clause ('but if'); برندارد is the present/subjunctive-negative third-person form of برداشتن ('he does not lift/take up') and sets the condition. پشیمان is a predicative adjective placed before the auxiliary شود, and شود (subjunctive/optative auxiliary) marks the resulting state: 'he becomes regretful.' The clause order is conditional → result.
segments.s504-g26: This is a purposive/result clause introduced by به + noun phrase: به هر درد دل = 'for/to every heart-pain' (درد دل = emotional pain/sorrow). سوی درمان شود uses سوی 'toward' + درمان 'remedy' and شود again as an auxiliary meaning 'is turned toward' or 'will turn to' — together: 'he will turn toward a cure for every heart's pain.' Word order: goal/direction (سوی+noun) follows the pain it answers.
segments.s504-g27: سپه (subject) + سوی (preposition 'toward') + آواز ('sound, voice') gives a directional phrase. بنهاد is past of نهادن ('placed/put') used idiomatically with گوش ('ear') — i.e., 'turned their ear to' or 'gave ear to' the sound. Here گوش functions as the object of بنهاد (set their ear).
segments.s504-g28: پراندیشه is a compound adjective meaning 'full of thought / worried'; شد is the copular/auxiliary 'became.' هرکسی here is distributive 'each/everyone' (subject), زان is a prepositional compound meaning 'from that / because of that', and خروش is the cause 'roar/cry.' Literal order: adjective+شد (predicate) followed by the subject and the causal prepositional phrase.
segments.s504-g29: که here introduces a relative/indefinite clause 'who/that (he) who'; بردارد is the present/subjunctive third-person of برداشتن ('lifts/takes up'). آن سنگ is the direct object 'that stone.' اگر بگذرد is a conditional subordinate clause 'if he passes/goes on' attached to the relative clause. Word order follows classical Persian: relative → verb → object → conditional.
segments.s504-g30: پی رنج = 'with regard to/after the suffering' (پی indicating relation/following), ناآمده is a compound negation + participle meaning 'not yet come / not come,' and نشمرد is the negative past/subjunctive of شمردن meaning 'he will not count/consider.' Together: 'he will not reckon the sufferings that have not come.'
segments.s504-g31: یکی گفت = 'someone said.' The following clause states a judgment: کین رنج هست از گناه. Here کین functions as a demonstrative/archaic determiner 'this/that' (not the sense 'vengeance' in the provided list), رنج is the subject 'suffering,' هست is the copula 'is/comes from,' and از گناه = 'from/because of sin.' Word order is subject-predicate with هست linking them.
segments.s504-g32: A simple nominal list: پشیمانی 'repentance/regret' linked by و 'and' to سنگ بردن (verbal noun phrase 'carrying a stone' or 'to carry a stone'); به راه is a prepositional phrase 'on the road / along the way' modifying سنگ بردن. The verb is omitted (predicate understood from the previous clause), so this enumerates the sources/kinds of suffering.
segments.s504-g33: دگر گفت = 'another said' with دگر (an indefinite 'another/one other') functioning as the subject before the reporting verb گفت. لختی بباید کشید is a compact clause: لختی = 'for a short while'; بباید is the impersonal modal 'it is necessary / one must' and is followed by the verb کشید (past-form of کشیدن) used here in a non-finite/quotative sense to mean 'endure' or 'bear.' The construction بباید + verb expresses obligation/necessity in classical Persian.
segments.s504-g34: مگر introduces a rhetorical/contrastive question here ('isn't it so? / after all'); درد و رنجش = 'pain and suffering' (two coordinated nouns). نباید + چشید uses the negative impersonal modal (نباید 'should/must not' in form) inside a rhetorical question meaning 'shouldn't one experience...' — چشید (here from چشیدن) means 'to experience/taste' (in the sense of undergoing something).
segments.s504-g35: یکی برد زان سنگ و دیگر نبرد is a compact narrative line: یکی برد = 'one (of them) took/carried (it)'; زان = poetic contraction of 'از آن' = 'from/that'; سنگ = 'stone' (here likely a jewel/precious stone). و دیگر نبرد — 'and another did not take (it)' uses نبرد as the negative past-form (ن + برد) meaning 'did not take/carry.' Word order is typical older Persian with the verb after its subject(s).
segments.s504-g36: یکی دیگر از کاهلی داشت خرد = 'another (one), from laziness, had [a] خرد.' از کاهلی is a prepositional/causal phrase 'because of/from laziness'; داشت is simple past 'had'; خرد here means 'wisdom, understanding' (so the clause says that this other person, through laziness, possessed little or insufficient understanding). Word order places the causal phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s504-g37: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; the phrase follows classical Persian order: چو از آب حیوان به هامون شدند = 'when (they) from the water, the animals, into the plain became' — i.e., 'when the animals came out of the water into the plain.' شدند is the past plural of شدن used here as an intransitive 'came/arrived.'
segments.s504-g38: ز تاریکی راه بیرون شدند = 'they came out of the darkness onto the path/road.' ز (poetic form of از) 'from'; تاریکی 'darkness'; راه 'road/path'; بیرون شدند literally 'became out' but idiomatically 'went/stepped out' — بیرون شدن + place = 'to emerge/go out to (a place).' Word order places the place-expression before the verbal phrase.
segments.s504-g39: بجستند هرکس بر و آستی = 'each one sprang up / leapt forward.' بجستند is past plural of بجَستن (to spring, leap, or start); هرکس 'each person/everyone' is the subject; بر (here an adverbial/particle meaning 'up/onward') and آستی is an adverb qualifying manner (in older usage, 'gently/quickly' depending on context). The clause describes simultaneous action by each individual.
segments.s504-g40: پدیدار شد کژی و کاستی = 'crookedness and deficiency appeared.' پدیدار شد is a two-word predicate meaning 'appeared / became manifest' (پدیدار + شد); کژی و کاستی are the abstract nouns 'wrongness/crookedness and lack/deficiency' and function as the things that became visible or evident.
segments.s504-g17: اِزان کوه با ناله آمد فرود = a prepositional phrase + manner phrase + verb+noun: ازان کوه ('from that mountain') is the source; با ناله ('with a moan') expresses manner; آمد فرود is a poetic sequence where آمد (came) and the noun فرود (descent/alighting) together mean 'came down/descended'. Word order is typical for Ferdowsi: adverbials before the verbal phrase.
segments.s504-g18: همی داد نیکی دهش را درود = همی is a habitual/progressive marker ('kept/continually'); داد is the main verb 'gave/offered'; نیکی دهش is a nominal phrase ('goodness–giving' = 'the one who gives goodness' or 'benefactor') and را marks that phrase as the definite/target of the action; درود (greeting/blessing) is the thing given and appears after the marked object for poetic order.
segments.s504-g19: بران راه تاریک بنهاد روی = بران ('thence/on that') is an adverbial link; راه تاریک is a noun + adjective ('the dark road'); بنهاد is the past of نهادن ('placed/turned'); روی here is 'face' and combines with نهادن in the verbal idiom رو نهادن ('to turn/set one's face toward'), so the phrase means 'he set/turned his face toward the dark road.'
segments.s504-g20: به پیش اندرون مردم راه‌جوی = به پیش ('forward/toward the front') indicates direction; اندرون مردم ('into the midst/inner part of the people') places him among people; راه‌جوی is a postpositive description 'the way-seeking/road-seeking people' (i.e. travelers) qualifying مردم. The whole phrase says he went forward into the crowd of travelers.
segments.s504-g21: چو آمد به تاریکی اندر سپاه = چو آمد ('when he came') introduces a temporal subordinate clause; به تاریکی ('into the darkness') and اندر سپاه ('among the host/army') are successive adverbial phrases specifying where he arrived. The verb آمد is simple past, and the two locative phrases follow it.
segments.s504-g22: خروشی برآمد ز کوه سیاه = خروشی (a roar/commotion) is the noun subject; برآمد ('arose/issued forth') is the verb; ز کوه سیاه ('from the black mountain') gives the source with poetic preposition ز. Word order (noun + verb + source) is natural in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s504-g23: که هرکس که بردارد از کوه سنگ = که introduces a relative/explanatory clause; هرکس که بردارد ('whoever lifts/takes up') uses بردارد in the present/subjunctive sense common in classical Persian for general statements; از کوه سنگ ('a stone from the mountain') is the object expressed with the from-phrase before the noun in this word order.
segments.s504-g24: پشیمان شود ز آنک دارد به چنگ = پشیمان شود ('will become regretful') uses شود as the future/resultative auxiliary; ز آنک here means 'because' ('for that/inasmuch as'); دارد به چنگ ('it has/is held by a claw') uses دارد (present 'has/holds') plus به چنگ as an instrumental/location phrase meaning 'in/under a claw' or 'held by a claw.' Together: '...will regret it, because it is held by a claw.'
segments.s504-g1: سکندر (subject) + چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause; بشنید is the past verb 'heard'. شد follows as a verb of change/motion ('became/turned'), so the whole string means 'when Alexander heard, he turned toward the mountain.' The directional phrase سوی کوه ('toward the mountain') follows the verb, typical of literary Persian word order where the result/state verb (شد) comes after the clause.
segments.s504-g2: به دیدار is a purposive/locative phrase 'to (go) meet / to the encounter'; بر تیغ شد is an idiomatic use where بر + noun + شد means 'took to the X' (here 'took to the sword' = drew/armed himself). بی‌گروه is an adverbial/qualifier 'without company / alone' modifying the action. The final شد is the past/resultive verb 'became/took on' completing the clause.
segments.s504-g3: سرافیل is the definite object marked by را; دید is the past verb 'saw/beheld'. صوری here describes the appearance/form (a substantive/adjectival sense) and is linked to به دست 'in/with his hand' (به functioning as an instrumental/locative 'by/with'), so the phrase means 'he saw Israfil, a form in his hand' (elliptical poetic phrasing).
segments.s504-g4: برافراخته is an adjectival/participial form 'raised/upright' modifying سر 'head'. ز جای is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from (his) place/seat' (ز = from), and نشست is the past verb 'sat'/'settled'. Together the clause conveys 'with head raised, he sat up from his place' (an idiomatic, compact description of alert motion).
segments.s504-g5: پر از X is the fixed pattern 'full of X' (پر = 'full'); from/of is expressed by از. باد is literal 'wind' here. لب دیدگان is a genitive construct 'the lip/edge of the eyes' (i.e. eye–rim), and پرزنم is a first-person singular present/jussive form 'I flutter / I take wing' used poetically. The sequence means 'I shall flutter, my lips/the rims of my eyes full of wind.'
segments.s504-g6: که introduces the following content clause ('that/when'); فرمان یزدان is 'the command/decree of God' (construct genitive). کی آید is the interrogative 'when will (it) come?' with آید present/future 'comes/will come'. The final که دم strings an emphatic noun 'what moment/breath' (دم here = 'moment'), so the whole rhetorical question asks 'when will God's command come — at what moment?'
segments.s504-g7: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بر کوه ('on the mountain') uses بر as the preposition 'on/upon', and روی here is the noun 'face/surface' making 'on the face of the mountain'; سکندر is the object/name; بدید is the past 'saw'. Word order is literary: temporal marker + locative phrase + object + verb = 'when (he) saw Alexander on the mountain's face.'
segments.s504-g8: The second چو functions as a comparative/simile 'as/like'; رعد خروشان is the noun + adjective 'roaring thunder'. فغان is 'a cry/lament', and برکشید (literally 'lifted up') is used idiomatically 'raised/uttered' (a cry). Together: 'like roaring thunder he raised a cry.'
segments.s504-g9: که‌ای is a vocative particle (که + ای) addressing someone directly ('O ...'); بنده آز is a nominal phrase 'slave/servant of greed' with noun + genitive modifier. چندین here functions adverbially/intensifying the verb ('many/a great deal'); مکوش is the negative imperative (archaic poetic form of مکو/مکوش) meaning 'do not strive/try'. Word order is vocative → addressee → adverbial modifier → negative imperative.
segments.s504-g10: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause here ('for/because' or 'that'), روزی is used adverbially ('one day/someday'), به + گوش = 'to (the) ear', آیدت is the 3rd-person present/future-like verb with enclitic object -ت 'comes to you' ('it comes to you' / 'befalls you'), and یک خروش = 'a single cry/roar' is the thing that may arrive. The clause explains the reason for the preceding prohibition.
segments.s504-g11: که here links the admonition to its content ('that/so'), چندین modifies the verb ('many times/repeatedly'), مرنج is a negative imperative/command meaning 'do not be upset/grieve', از پی = 'on account of / from the pursuit of' (از + پی 'after/in pursuit of'), and تاج و تخت = 'crown and throne' (objects of the pursuit). The phrase warns against being repeatedly distressed over ruling honors.
segments.s504-g12: به رفتن = 'for leaving / in order to go' (به + مصدر رفتن), بیارای here is an imperative or causative sense 'make ready / array yourself (for the departure)' in the poetic context (the provided lemma senses don't match perfectly, so this is an idiomatic reading), و = 'and', بربند رخت is an imperative 'bind/pack up your clothes/baggage' (بربند + رخت). Together the clause instructs preparation for departure.
segments.s504-g13: چنین = 'thus' (manner adverb), داد is the past of دادن 'gave' (here 'gave/sent'), پاسخ = 'answer/reply', and بدو = 'to him' (به + او contracted). شهریار is the subject 'the king'. Word order is adverb → verb → object → indirect object → subject, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s504-g14: که introduces the content of the reply ('that' / 'saying that'), بهر من = 'for me' / 'on my account' (بهر = for/toward), این آمد = 'this came' (آمد = came/happened; این = this), از روزگار = 'from fate/time' (از = from; روزگار here = fate/time). This is the reported content of the king's answer.
segments.s504-g15: که again introduces a clause ('that' / 'for'), جز = 'except', جنبش و گردش = 'movement and rotation' (two nouns joined by و), اندر جهان = 'in the world' (اندر = in/within; جهان = world/life). The clause sets up what the speaker perceives as the only realities in the world.
segments.s504-g16: نبینم is 1st-person present negative 'I do not see' (negated verb), همی is an archaic/poetic particle meaning 'still/indeed' or used for emphasis, آشکار و نهان = 'manifest/clear and hidden' (two adjectives joined by و). Together: 'I see nothing ... manifest or hidden.'

sentences.s504-s21: Beside one (of them) was something full of rubies; another was full of imperishable gems.
sentences.s504-s22: He who had little became regretful; with a peridot he so stuck in a thorn.
sentences.s504-s23: Still more regretful is the one who did not take (for himself); from that costly gem he set (an ornament) upon his head.
sentences.s504-s24: They stayed at that spot for two weeks; when the army became more at ease, it moved on.
sentences.s504-s13: But if he does not lift it, he will be regretful; for every pain of the heart he will turn toward a cure.
sentences.s504-s14: The host turned its ear toward the sound; everyone, because of that cry, became full of thought (anxious).
sentences.s504-s15: Whoever lifts that stone — if he goes on — will not count the sufferings that have not yet come.
sentences.s504-s16: Someone said, 'This suffering comes from sin: remorse, and carrying the stone on the road.'
sentences.s504-s17: Another said, "One must endure a little; after all, should one not experience pain and suffering?"
sentences.s504-s18: One took that stone and another did not take (it); another one, out of laziness, had little understanding.
sentences.s504-s19: When the beasts came out of the water into the plain, they emerged from the darkness onto the road.
sentences.s504-s20: Each sprang up (or set off), and soon crookedness and deficiency appeared.
sentences.s504-s9: He came down from that mountain with a moan, and kept offering greetings to the giver of good.
sentences.s504-s10: Thence he turned his face toward the dark road and went forward into the midst of the traveling people.
sentences.s504-s11: When he came into the darkness among the host, a roar rose from the black mountain.
sentences.s504-s12: For whoever lifts a stone from the mountain will be sorry, because it is held by a claw.
sentences.s504-s1: When Alexander heard, he turned toward the mountain; he went to meet (it) and took up his sword alone.
sentences.s504-s2: He saw Israfil, form in hand; with his head uplifted he sat up from his place.
sentences.s504-s3: My lips and the rims of my eyes I will fill with wind and flutter; when will God's command come — at what moment?
sentences.s504-s4: When he saw Alexander on the face of the mountain, like roaring thunder he raised a cry.
sentences.s504-s5: O slave of greed, do not strive so much, for one day a single cry may come to your ear.
sentences.s504-s6: Do not be upset repeatedly in pursuit of crown and throne; prepare to leave and bundle up your belongings.
sentences.s504-s7: The king thus answered him: 'This has come to me from fate.'
sentences.s504-s8: I see nothing in the world except movement and rotation, manifest and hidden.
